speaker: interviewer
text: Faigie, we were talking about your boyfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first boyfriend. And it was very interesting how it also brings out how my mother was so protective over me, because he hit me with a snowball after the dining room. My mother marched in, made such a ruckus. His father beat him. How dare he touch her little girl! And from then on, I never squealed on anybody in my whole life. And we became the best of friends. We were like good friends. I really liked his brother better. And that was the life. We studied. We enjoyed. It was the DP camp. And we were waiting. But everybody was waiting to move out of Europe, to leave Europe. That was on everybody's day and night dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Zionist groups that came into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Formed Zionist groups, sure. A lot of people went to Israel. Of course, we nearly went also. If my mother's sisters would not have insisted that I wouldn't have anybody-- a lot of people went to the States. A lot of people went-- some people went to South Africa. I think slower South Africa, I don't know. Canada and Israel, naturally Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were happy and life was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --looking a lot more rosy for you. Did you have nightmares about your experiences during the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nightmares. But I suppose there I started to play that game, it didn't happen to me. I wanted to forget it. I wanted to forget it, because I remember other people talking about it. I did not, because you see, maybe the trauma was there with my father, that I lost my father and that part of life was finished, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because that was an-- an unusual, wonderful relationship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I suppose I stopped talking. I didn't talk at all about what happened. I went on and waited, went to school there. We studied. We sang. I danced ballet. We had performances on the stage. I love to perform. I love to sing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and made a lot of friends, a lot of friends, because being an only child, very good friends. But everybody-- we knew it's not going to be a forever friendship, because everybody wanted to leave, no matter how good it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people applied. Some went to Israel, like I said before. Which papers came first, you left. We were from the-- quite the first ones to leave in March of 1948. And when did you arrive in Canada? How did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat-- a Greek boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, by train to Montreal. My mother's sister Nahama-- Nina, she was called-- Nina [? Feinbloom ?] and her husband, they made our papers. He did a lot. He tried very hard as soon as possible to make out papers. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your feeling arriving in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exhilarated. I had very high hopes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I was in March 13, turned 14 in July. Right away-- they lived in NDG, in a very nice part of Montreal. They enrolled me-- don't forget it was March. It was after the spring vacation, so they enrolled me in a English public school. And I started to study, having the background of wanting to learn. Day and night-- I say that there, I spoiled my eyes. Day and night, I listened to the radio and day and night, I studied. And when I-- at the end of June, when I read to the class-- the teacher was very smart. She didn't-- she didn't bother me. Everybody stood up and clapped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there were not many survivors of your age.

speaker: interviewee
text: There weren't many survivors. There was nobody in that-- in NDG, in that-- in Wellington public school, there was nobody. I was the only one. I suppose the teacher explained that I'm coming from Europe and I cannot read yet. Everybody was very helpful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when-- when I read-- as I said before, I graduated public school in 1948 and went on to high school. And I did very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you mix mainly with Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jew-- non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Yiddishkeit was very important to me. I mixed mainly with Jewish children, with Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you and your mother live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt and uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did that continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four months we lived with them. And my mother went naturally to the Jewish General to apply for a job. They told her no. You can't-- you can be like an orderly or you can be an assistant. My mother should be an assistant. You are not allowed to give a needle. My mother decided to go back to school. In the meantime, it was the summer of '49-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no. The summer of '48. And my mother went to-- became a nurse at a children's camp. They allowed her that, Camp Kinderwelt. My uncle was very into organizations. My mother became a nurse. And she met-- I was a camper, naturally. She met her future husband there. He was an artist and he was-- his specialty was landscapes. He was never married before. He was the sweetest man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Israel Malmaud. Everybody knew him. He was a very kind-- he was a teacher before, a Hebrew teacher. But he gave that up and he became a [NON-ENGLISH] only to make a living for the interim while he can paint. His paintings were his children. There are some paintings here. My mother's house was covered. He used to go away for the summer and paint. That's how he met my mother. And I decided that I'm going to like him, that he's going to be-- he's not my father but he will be, because a friend of mine-- I'll never forget it. The mother remarried. She ran away from home. And I said, I'm going to see. And he was a darling. We became the best of friends. I had the best sandwiches in high school when my mother had salami, thick ones. I used to share it with-- and carry it on the side, because it used to smell from the garlic. But I had in high school the best sandwiches. He used to make me sandwiches. We became the best of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained an only child after they married.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained an only child. My mother, I'll tell you, must have been too busy. It was a very big undertaking. She had to go to hospitals to work, to get practical-- my mother, who was a nurse all her life, had to get practical experience. She went to McGill to study the language. And when she graduated, she did better than a lot of the young girls. And they used to ask her at report time in the hospital to spell words. Now, she changed from surgical nurse, pediatrics. She became at the Jewish General a pediatric nurse in pediatrics.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your relationship with your mother at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was OK then. It was-- I was very proud of her. I was still-- my mother was still the boss. I never slept at a girlfriend's house. I never rode a bicycle. I never went skating, because God forbid something would happen to me. I studied. I didn't stay out late like the normal that the kids did. The first time I stayed out late was New Year's Eve and phoned my mother. And at a party, I met this gorgeous hunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Benny Libman. I was in school. I wasn't allowed to date during the week. We don't date during the week. We date only Saturday and Sunday. So he used to-- he met me at a friend's house. I went to a party. And he kept on asking me out, but I was always very busy and-- like, busy with schoolwork and going out to-- oh, I'm not saying. But I never liked to-- he used to ask me not long in advance. I never liked to break a date. But then, I suppose he decided, and as fate has it, he asked me out for New Year's Eve six weeks in advance. Now, I wanted to go and visit my friends in Toronto for New Year's vacation, because as I said, [? Rachmiel ?] went to the states. But I had a very good friend, Willie [? Mole, ?] who moved here. I had his uncle and his aunt, the [? Morchatzkas, ?] that I was very close. They thought-- I was like their little kid. I wanted to visit Toronto. I heard of Toronto. I wanted to visit. My mother said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she heard that I had a date, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- she was very happy that I had a date for New Year's Eve. And I decided to go out with Benny because I really liked him. He was dark, tall, with a nice white shirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him another time at his-- at a girlfriend. He was going out with also at a party-- never allowed to go out during the week. And New Year's Eve, we went out for the first time. And Benny kept on asking me Saturday for Sunday and Sunday for Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take until you were a steady relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: A steady? A while, because my mother nixed the relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She liked Benny very much. As you know, I do come from a whole professional lineage. Benny comes from regular Jews, wonderful people. He was saved in Siberia, his mother, his father, and three sisters. He had the nicest father. I loved my mother-in-law. I-- as a-- I used to come there and they used to-- my-- my-- my father-in-law, my late father-in-law, the moment Benny brought me-- like, on a second date, he brought me home. He took him aside and he said, I like her. Hold onto her. And my name is Faigie. My mother-in-law's name is Faigie Miriam. He went to a rabbi to ask-- because as you know, the Ashkenazi, we don't have the same names-- to ask Rabbi [? Hirschbon, ?] the head of the rabbinical institute in Montreal, if it is OK. But luckily her name was Miriam also, my mother-in-law, my future mother-in-law. And I started to go out with Benny while in school, kept up all my grades. That was the first disobedience that I disobeyed my mother, starting to go steady with Benny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you and Benny finally married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Engaged nearly three years later, married three and a half years later, because it was a no-no. I met Benny at the age of 16. I had-- also before Benny, I had a very, very nice boyfriend who got killed on the-- on the road. He was crossing the street. My mother liked him very much. He was a dentist and also a dentist technician. A very nice man-- Nathan [? Birkau. ?] He was very nice. He got killed on the-- so I was like, I met Benny. I was sort of looking for a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you established a new life in Montreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montreal, a completely new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you and Benny subsequently have?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I graduated from high school, did very well. And in Montreal, if you know, you have to do both subjects. And I did very well and got a fantastic job, even trial balance. I went to a commercial high school. I was supposed to go to university, like, for higher learning. And I met Benny. And I went-- we got married. And I got a fantastic job at [INAUDIBLE] transport custom brokers. I was their chief bookkeeper through the paper. I was-- and I have to mention that, because that was a real coup. Here is this greeny, got a fantastic position. Got my other friend from school [? in Gita ?] to work. And we both managed a whole set of books. And I married. I married Benny then. And as fate has it, three months after being married, I became pregnant. And I worked 'til March. I had Philip. And to this day, I was very-- I loved my mother-in-law, because she was very excited. Benny and I-- I was supposed to work. I was supposed to amass, because I did very well at work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you and Benny have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four, Philip, Jonah, Lenny, and the baby was Ann. Princess Ann was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have now 10 [NON-ENGLISH]. Philip has four in Israel and Jonah has three girls and a boy, and Lenny has two.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll seen them in the photographs that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you're going to show us later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you why you and Benny moved from Montreal to Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved from Montreal to Toronto. We had a very good life in Montreal. Benny had a transport company. And he was doing well. But with the Pierre Laporte business, being old refugees, the turmoil with the FLQ was not for our liking. It took us a whole year to decide. You have to understand that my kids went to private school in those days at Herzliah. In 1968, I had a bunch of kids at school that-- but we had to pick up everything and move to Toronto because we were afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? When did you come to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September of '72. One of Benny's companies told him to sell his business and come to work for them, so he went in May of '72.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what has been your occupation since coming to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since coming to Toronto, I was-- after-- in Montreal, I used to do books. Naturally, the kids were small. I wouldn't leave my children. I didn't have any sisters or brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to help out at school. And one of the teachers, a Mrs. Fields said to me, Mrs. Libman, what do you do? So I told her. She says, you're a natural. Why don't you become a teacher? So I went and I studied at night. It took about three years. And I started in Montreal with the Y in [INAUDIBLE] as a nursery school teacher. And when I moved in '72 and my son Philip went on a program the first time to Israel-- because he was-- I instilled in them the love for Israel-- he kept on saying, you have to continue it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very lucky that with my credentials, I was given a chance at Bialik. I worked at Bialik for two years. And when-- after Bialik, Associated heard of me. And I went to sub there. And Mrs. Kurtz and Miss Nagel liked me very much. And I started to work as a junior kindergarten teacher. And I've been there ever since for the past 20 years. And it's my joy. This is-- whenever I have a problem and I go to school, I forget about everything. I-- this is my-- I was supposed to do it. I love it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you a few final questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With regard to your experiences during the war, in what way do you think this has affected you as a parent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a teacher, as a caregiver for children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. First of all, for my kids, I never went-- I'm sure a lot of parents do the same thing. I'm not unique. I never closed my eyes until everybody was home. OK. It took an awful lot for me-- I let-- he was 17, Philip, to let him go to Israel. But I instilled in them the love. I never went to Israel 'til 1980. But I instilled in them the love for Jewishness, the love for brotherhood, the love for getting along with all kinds of people. Philip, had a lot of non-Jewish friends too. Professors were his friends to this day. But I instilled in them the love for Israel. And Philip, my mother blamed me for letting Philip go. But he-- maybe from my talk and the love of learning, Jonah went to-- to university in Ottawa. He wanted to be a big fish in a smaller pond. And he graduated as a biologist. Lenny remained in Toronto and Ann also in Toronto. And Jonah moved to Ottawa. And then when he got married, he moved back to-- he moved to Toronto. And Philip came back on a sabbatical here. And Philip finished University of Toronto as a computer-- no, as a economist and in urban planning, because he wanted to move to Israel. He knew-- he told me, I will-- I will definitely live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you-- one of the reasons that you have given your testimony today is to tell your story to your children. Do you have a message for your children, for your grandchildren, for the future generations who will be watching this testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose so. To be good people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it's very hard at this moment. Can we break for a minute? This is why I love school and I try to teach all my kids. I've been there 20 years and I used to work a whole day, so that means that I had about 50 kids a year. And then I worked a half a day, so I had about 25. I'd like to instill in them to get along, to be nice to other people, and to remember that they're Jews, that this never should happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my mother and I. I don't know how old I am, but it's 1935 it says in the back of the picture. And it must be winter or early fall, because look how nice she's dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kovno. Yeah. Now, this as you can see, is me and my mother, my mother and I, in Krakinova. It is summer vacation. And I think all those pictures my aunt sent me after the war, because, as you know, we didn't have anything left. This is a celebration, must've been a birthday. Maybe it was even my birthday. You see me all dressed up. It's in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you, Faigie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right there to the-- yeah. This is me. Yeah. And it was in 1938, just-- I was four years old. Look at this. Look how cute. Also in Krakinova, because you can see it's like a-- it's not like a cosmopolitan city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you vacationed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your maternal grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my maternal grandparents. Look how cute. Look at the bow in my hair. And can you see the nice clothes I had? And my mother too. You see, remember I told you I lived on my clothes. We lived on our clothes. That is. You can see this good-looking man on the right is my father. Next is his mother. And-- but the one with the hat also is my paternal grandfather. And the rest are my father's sisters. And my mother is there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in 1932 or '33 at the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kovno, in Lithuania, because afterwards, the sisters emigrated. This is a document given to me in Ebelsberg, the second DP camp. I was in-- in Bad Gastein and Ebelsberg from November 1945 to March 1948, 'til I came to Canada, to Montreal. This is at our wedding, June 20th, 1954. And this is Benny Norman Libman. And I call him [NON-ENGLISH]. And he happens to be one of the nicest men that walks this earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is my pride and joy, my son Philip. On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. Next to him is Jonah, and the other gentleman is Lenny. And Princess Ann is right in the middle. OK. On the top is my son Lenny and his beautiful wife Debra. On the left is my son Jonah and his also beautiful wife Lila. And on the right is my son Philip and his beautiful wife Tamar. They live in Israel. This is a picture of Benny and I in 1989 with eight of our grandchildren, [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is my other two grandchildren. [? Yael ?] lives in Israel now. And that's-- the other one is [? Adena. ?] This picture is my mother and my stepfather. He actually was my-- just like my real father. It was taken in Toronto in 1990 at my son Lenny's wedding and Debbie's. And this is also I'm very lucky. I have more pride and joy. This is my Associated Hebrew School's class graduation, junior kindergarten, 1995. I'm at the left. And on the right is my very able assistant Sharon Rock. And they were wonderful. We had a wonderful year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture they presented to me at graduation on a t-shirt. And all the names-- you can't see, but all the names are in the back. Now, this is a very auspicious picture. It's from Anne Frank. And this is the time when I started to talk about my experience. I never talked about anything. My children only knew that I was a newcomer to this country, but they never knew anything. I started to talk about when it first in 1982 appeared an article in the paper that Helmut Rauca was the first Nazi criminal arrested and extradited to Germany. And this was the same Rauca that I-- I had the experience. So this is when I started at a-- we had the Anne Frank exhibit. And I started to talk in 1989 about my experiences to schoolchildren, especially schoolchildren. And I have the same message that she had. In spite of everything, I still believe that people are really good at heart. And this is more for my reason for living-- my daughter Lila and my daughter Ann and my granddaughter [? Rakhelia ?] and my granddaughter [INAUDIBLE] and my grandson [? Avi ?] [? Avraham ?] and my granddaughter [? Eliza ?] [? Nava. ?]
